speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 5, Helen Jonas Rosenzweig. Were you-- did you see him shoot Adam?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was in the yard. I heard the shots. I heard the shots. And I ran, I ran halfway, and one of my friend grab me in his arm. He said, you're goin' to be all right. You're goin' to be all right. I had a feeling that it must have been Adam, because he was looking for him. And he ordered to-- after he was laying there, with his eyes open, my friend said his eyes were open when he shot him. He was in a shock. Because he trusted this Ukrainian. And then Goeth ordered to hang him-- to have Adam hanged. And my sisters told me, they called the whole camp to be assembled, to watch him hanging. And over the-- over him was a sign, whoever will try to keep any arms or try to escape, this is what's waiting for him or her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] He was a brave young boy who really tried so hard and worked on it. But it didn't succeed. I feel that I had participated in this situation. Unfortunately, never came to it, because they had a plan that was acted out very well and so little we could do about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, why did you get involved with helping him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I felt-- just like I have a responsibility and duty to speak about it now, at that time I felt that I, I have to do it. Because I'm in position to get those informations. And, you know, the minute I saw him closing the door, and halfway down the road, I would have run upstairs. And you know that I spoke to Helen in Israel. She didn't even know about it. That's how I kept it a secret. But I really-- first I didn't know what I was looking for. I didn't really read that well, fluently, German, to know what I was-- but, but it was given to me. Adam gave me such specific instruction and details, where and how, that it took me four and a half days. And it wasn't one day after the other. Whenever I had a chance, it took me a long time. But whenever Helen was very busy with something and I knew he's gone, I would run to the-- there was, there was one room that was full of tobacco and arms hanging on the walls. It was a panelled room, with wooden panelled room. And there was a large cabinet. In that cabinet were all kind of rolled-up documents, all kinds of folders. And I was just looking, looking through. I guess I was lucky to find what they were looking for. And I had, like, a tiny camera that Adam gave me that I, I made the photos from it. You know, I put it aside completely. I didn't even want to remember about it. But when a friend of mine called me and said, Helen, there is a book written, and you in the book, and Adam is on few pages of the book. And he sent me the book. And actually, since that time, when I made the other tape for the Heritage Museum, this was the time I started to talk about it. Because it's actually written, black and white. So otherwise it would be unbelievable, that anyone my age would take a chance like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you-- did Goeth say anything to you after, after he killed Adam?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because everything was happening very, very quick, very rapidly. The deportation were going very fast. They were moving people out. They were trying to liquidate camp Plaszow, as I mentioned to you before. The Russians are moving, moving closer and closer. And the liquidation began. And till one day, there was a doorbell. And I came to open the door. And there were two civilians-- German-- asked for Amon Goeth. I went upstairs, and I said, Herr Kommandeur-- I explain him that somebody's looking for him. He came down. I came down toward the kitchen. But I was standing on the last step and sneaking, looking, you know, seeing what's happening. It was kind of different normally that anybody is at two people asking for him, two men. And I saw them showing some kind of a paper. And that moment, I saw him reaching for his hat, and his belt, with his gun. And he walked in the middle, out of the villa, with those two men.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ran into the kitchen. I said, I said, Helen, you know, two, two, two Germans came, civilian. They asked for him. And when he came down, he, he walked in the middle. You saying they taking him away? She says, [NON-ENGLISH], you're such a child. Who could take him away? He's the, he's the kommandeur. He-- who could possibly order him to go out? You child. You're so childish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't know-- they showed him paper. And he walked, like, in the middle. And this is what happened. We never saw him again. We only heard, later, that he asked for some underwear to be sent. Packed up some underwear and socks and somebody's going to pick it up. Rumors-- somebody was saying later that he did leave for Vienna, where he actually--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this, when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, like, beginning of October.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the civilians took him away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he asked for underwear how much later?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was maybe like five days later. Not exactly. Maybe it was a week. Close to a week. Something around that time. Because we were, like, two in the villa. We didn't know what to do. And we just thought maybe he will come back. But she disappeared. She wasn't there anymore. She left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were alone. And-- but the camp was getting empty, because they were transporting people out. You know, it was a liquidation, you know. And they were burning, burning corpses, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened, what we found out later, that the Germans became very jealous of his fortune. Because he was taking a lot of the belongings of the prisoners and taking for his own personal possession. You know that they were actually pulling out gold teeth from, from already murdered people? People that were already dead, they ordered a dentist to pull out gold teeth from their mouth. Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I was told. Later. I didn't see anything, because I was only in the villa. But all those things, people knew about those things. And evidently what happened is that the Germans denounce him to the authorities that he is stealing, rather than giving everything to the authorities. And, as a result, they arrested him. And we were free. And, you know, from nowhere, suddenly, there comes Schindler. And I, I see him standing in the door. There were few steps coming to the villa. He-- I thought he wanted to come in. He didn't come in. He's standing there on the steps, you know, his hands in a pocket. And he said to me, Susanna, you coming with me. I, I looked at him. He said, I have a list. I never knew about a list. I didn't even know what he's talking about. He says, I built a factory in Czechoslovakia. And you coming with me. I was, like, stunned. But, all of a sudden, everything he told me before started to be put together. Because he always used to tell me, you will survive. I will help you. And here he is!

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, you have family? Oh, if I would have my family, he would take probably everybody. I said, I do have only two sisters. He said, give me the names! And at that moment I was thinking about my cousins that were in that transport, my aunts, my grandmother. You know, one day I came to visit my mother. And I saw that her bunk was, like, very high, you know, with the, with the little quilt what they had. I said, Mom, what is it so high? She says, it's my mother, my grandmother. I said, what are you doing? She says, they taking all older people. I have to save her. I said, Mom, but, but they going to kill you. She said, but that's my mother. You know, those things come back to me. You know, there's so much-- so many details. But one cannot remember everything, you know? I don't really remember that much, only the things that stand out most in my mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was standing there, and I gave him the names of my sisters. And he said to me, listen. If you have anything that you want to save, give it to me. I give it to you in Czechoslovakia. Because you're going to be in Auschwitz. It's going to be a quarantine. You're going to stay there. They're going to take everything away from you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he knew that, that you were-- that the people on the list were going to go to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: He probably assumed that this is how it's going to happen. Because in the movie it says that it was derailed. But he told me, and I went down. I remember my valise. It was a greyish little valise. I remember what I put in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you put in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I put in my father's gold watch with a chain. I had a, I had a chain with a beautiful gold heart from Adam. I had my mother's one diamond [POINTING] earring, yet, and, and, and her ring with, like, a butterfly with diamonds. And I put my Persian hat and my leather boots and my coat in there. I remember exactly what I put in there. I never have gotten it back. But it didn't matter. He told me, it got lost. In Brunnlitz, when I approached him a couple times, I said, Herr Schindler. Oh, [NON-ENGLISH], he would say to me, it got lost. So it got lost. Probably got lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the difference? I was alive. My sisters were alive. It didn't matter. Were you able to bring anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take anything with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? To Auschwitz? Yeah, I was dressed. I, I took dress. All of us had something. What happened is, we got into the-- to Auschwitz. I remember arriving at night. I remember walking into Auschwitz. It was very quiet. And we walked, like, in fives. For me, even if it was Auschwitz, it felt safe. Because I was with everybody else. Because, you know, I must tell you that many times when we were told to sleep in the other house, I stayed near that window of mine and watched the people marching to work. I envy them. Because, you know, I knew they're going through daily hell. They were killing, there were 25 lashes, which my sister got twice. They were living in daily hell, but they were together. And I was all alone there in that horrible villa with that monster upstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- what was the trip like from Plaszow to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. It was very close, very close. And, you know, it was very, very strange, because the air smelled funny, you know? And all we saw is the chimneys. And everybody was, like, looking up. But nobody said anything. But we looked looked at the chimney. There was smoke coming up. And, you know, the sky looked like red a little bit. You know, like, between the air and the sky, it was reddish and funny smell. And very silent. And we marched, marched closer to the chimneys. And we went around the chimneys. And then finally we ended up in a room. And there were SS men standing, and they said, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. Means, fast, fast, fast. Get undressed. Get undressed and throw it on the pile. And we started get undressed. And we're standing there and standing there. And there were some Slovak women there that worked there. And they said, oh, probably in the morning there'll be nothing left from you. Because the crematorium, the ovens, were right next to it. It was a shower and the crematorium. But, you know, at that point-- I never said it to anybody-- but at that point, I tell you, I knew we're going to the shower. Because I knew what Schindler was telling me through the times he was coming to the villa. The fact that he came [?through?] for me, I felt not feared. I didn't fear. Now, I found out most of my friends that were there, the 300 women, they thought that, oh, this is be the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the whole three weeks, I felt that he is going to save us. I, somehow, knowing what he told me-- and he did come for me-- because I knew nothing about a list. I wouldn't even know. I probably would be taken away, like the rest of the women and people from camp. And who knows where?

speaker: interviewee
text: However, he came for me. And he even asked me for my family to be saved. So I knew that he must have a pull. And I started to put two and two together, that his visiting Goeth had probably a purpose. And it started to dawn on me that he is working like a double agent. In my little head, things started to be clear, that he was there with them because he used them. And he was a really decent human being. Whatever his, whatever his motivation was, but down to the bottom he was a decent human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Auschwitz for those three weeks with the other women on Schindler's list, were, were you, were you able-- were you talking about him? Were they talking about him differently than you? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we did talk. You know, Auschwitz was a horrible experience. You know, Auschwitz was built for death. Everything you saw in Auschwitz, you knew you're there to die. The barracks had no windows. The washing-room has ice-cold water. There was no soap. There was no, no glass in the window. Everything was meant, meant to die. The mud was, like, almost over the half of your calves. When we going back to the shower, we were standing. They told us to throw everything away. Somehow, we all had a cake of soap in our hands. I don't know when it was given to us, but we had a cake of soap. I'm standing with that cake of soap in my hand, and I'm holding the last bunch of pictures in my hand. And I'm looking at the pile, and I'm naked. And I said, if I throw this away, I will never see the faces of my parents, ever. And I started to look through the pictures so quickly. And I found a couple of photos of, like, for, for passports. And those with the largest faces of all the other pictures. And I started to tear around the faces. As a matter of fact, I have one original that I took of my boyfriend as well. This is the size of that picture that I tear around. And I tried very quickly to dig a hole in the soap to put those three faces.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, looking in front of me, I saw one of the SS men shaving a girl's hair. And with his rifle, he hit her. And the soap went flying. And I said, oh, what if he does that to me? I'm losing my pictures! And I turned around very quickly, and I stuck it in my mouth.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I must tell you something. 30 years later, one of my friends came to visit me in Wantagh. I lived  on Long Island. And I had those pictures blown up and made them larger. And I had them framed on the wall. And my friend looked at the pictures, and she says, Helen, I was standing in Auschwitz behind you when I saw you putting that in your mouth. 30 years later. So I had a witness. Finally, we're going through the showers. My older sister always was watching over me. Since I lost my father and mother, she was the one that always took care of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she looked at me-- she says, are you all right, are you all right, while the water was coming down. I said, mm. She says, what's the matter with you? Are you OK? I couldn't talk. I had those pictures in my mouth. And as soon as we came out, I said, I'm OK. But I have the pictures in my mouth-- Mom, Dad, Adam. She says, why would you do a thing like that? They would kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had my pictures. And we have the faces of our parents. For I'm grateful, otherwise I wouldn't even remember how they look, probably. They gave us little dresses that were sheer, nothing. No underwear, no socks, no stockings, little Dutch clogs. It was freezing. End of November in Poland was very, very cold, very cold. It was freezing. There was nothing whatsoever for a girl, for a sanitary needs for her. There was no toilet paper, nothing whatsoever. It was so cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have healthy periods?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lost it later. They supposedly put something in the coffee. From the beginning, we did, but then something happened. And people were saying that they do put something in the coffee to stop the period. [SIGHS] In the morning, we were taken to Birkenau. And we were examined by Dr. Mengele. They called him the Angel of Death. He told us to take off  the little sheer dress, looked us over. The one that didn't look so healthy went to one side. They asked the age. He asked the age. To be honest with you, from the three weeks that I was in Birkenau, I think that we were undressed maybe three, four, times-- three times. But we also were called out in the middle of the night outside in the freezing cold to be counted. And we couldn't even hold onto each other, because they were counting us while they were searching the barracks. I remember one incident where I was trembling, and my teeth were jittering it was so cold. And I looked up in the grey sky. [CRYING] And I thanked God. [SOBBING] I thanked God that my mother was spared. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was so cold. And we also saw people in Auschwitz, other women, that must have been there a long time. They looked so dreadful in those tattered clothes, in this shabby clothes, so skinny, with shaven heads. Sometimes they would steal this little soupy cereal, you know. It was like-- they gave us like a watery cereal. It was like water, practically. They would grab from us, they were so hungry. I guess they couldn't control them self. And some times later, I heard some of our-- of the 300 people-- would say, you know, maybe this is what's going to happen when we, when we remain here longer. But I always kept saying, Schindler would do something. Schindler would do-- but, naturally, you could never tell what can happen. The three weeks were, were very difficult. Because we couldn't even eat what we got. Because we developed dysentery. And we were ill.

speaker: interviewee
text: So fortunately, the three weeks-- they put us all together, and they called us by our names, which I understand it never happened in history of Auschwitz, that they would call prisoners by their name. They usually had numbers. But we were not numbered. And they called us by our name.

speaker: interviewee
text: All they did it is, like, they smeared us with a paintbrush before we got on those trains. Because I, I think the reason was, in case somebody tried to escape, we would be easily spotted, recognized, you know. When we arrived in Brunnlitz, I remember one of my legs-- that's why maybe I have trouble with it-- was frozen, because it was very cold and we were completely not dressed. I mean, I had this sheer dress on, nothing more. I remember friends carrying me to the camp. When we came into the camp-- Brunnlitz-- there was Schindler, standing there with SS men, SS women. And he had a smile. And he said, you're finally here. The men were there already. But we came later. The factory was not finished. But we slept on the floor, on the straw, next to one another. But we didn't care. We knew Schindler is there. And I especially knew what he told me. I felt safe. I felt Goeth wasn't around anymore. And no matter where I, Auschwitz, Brunnlitz, I felt I was together with everybody else. And he cannot harm me anymore. Brunnlitz was, to explain to you, to me, I recall a large factory. What I did in factory, it was almost every day the same thing. Nothing moved. Nothing changed. I couldn't understand what was going on. All I knew, that I saw Schindler coming through the factory, always with a pleasant expression. There was no beating. There was no killing. We were not really hungry-hungry. I had some friends who would go out to work who would bring me back potatoes. And I don't remember-- I don't remember being hungry. I mean, it wasn't that great, but I wasn't hungry. And we didn't fear anybody, because we knew Schindler is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll continue about that on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [SIGHS]
